evals.name: Dipole on Open Plate with MoM-PO Solver
evals.description: Tests core workflow: platform with explicit units, installedAntenna setup, MoM-PO as default solver for open platforms, and mesh density appropriate for MoM-PO
evals.prompt: Install a dipole antenna at 1 GHz on a 1m x 1m metal plate. Position it 10 cm above the plate center. Use the appropriate solver for this configuration. Show the geometry, mesh, and compute the radiation pattern.

assertions.description: Generated code runs without error in MATLAB
Uses MoM-PO solver (correct default for large open plate)
assertions.expected: SolverType set to "MoM-PO" for the installed antenna. The skill teaches that MoM-PO is the default solver for large open platforms (plates, vehicle panels). Must NOT use "MoM" (O(N^2) memory, prohibitive for large platforms) or "FMM" (overkill for a simple open plate with no concave features).

assertions.description: Sets platform Units explicitly to meters
assertions.expected: Platform geometry uses meters as the working unit. Either platform() is created with Units="m" explicitly, OR geometry is generated programmatically with vertices in meters and Units="m" set when loading the STL. The skill teaches that platform defaults to mm but ElementPosition is always in meters. Must NOT leave Units as default mm when geometry is in meters.

assertions.description: ElementPosition places antenna 0.1m above plate in the z-coordinate
assertions.expected: ElementPosition set with z-coordinate of 0.1 (meters). The position [x y 0.1] places the dipole 10cm above the plate center. Must NOT use z=10 (that would be 10 meters, a unit mismatch) or z=100 (mm value not converted).

assertions.description: Meshes the installed antenna before computing pattern
assertions.expected: mesh() is called on the installed antenna before pattern() or impedance(). The skill teaches to always mesh explicitly before analysis. Must NOT skip meshing — uncontrolled mesh density leads to inaccurate results, especially for MoM-PO where lambda/6 is the guideline.

evals.name: Two-Element Coupling with S-Parameters
evals.description: Tests multi-element workflow: cell array for Element, matching ElementPosition rows, S-parameter coupling analysis, platform Units
evals.prompt: Install two dipole antennas at 2 GHz on a metal plate, separated by 20 cm along the x-axis, both 8 cm above the plate. Analyze the isolation between them using S-parameters over a 10% bandwidth (use 21 frequency points).

assertions.description: Generated code runs without error in MATLAB
Computes S-parameters for isolation/coupling analysis
assertions.expected: The output demonstrates S-parameter analysis for the two-element antenna system. Evidence includes: calling sparameters() on the installed antenna, plotting or displaying S21/S12 (coupling/isolation), or showing an S-parameter matrix. Must NOT skip S-parameter computation — the user explicitly asked for isolation analysis which requires sparameters().

assertions.description: Multi-element setup uses cell array Element with 2 positions
assertions.expected: The installed antenna is configured with multiple elements: Element property is a cell array containing 2 antenna entries, and ElementPosition is a 2-row matrix placing them 20cm apart along x-axis at 8cm height. The code must run without error (assertion a1 verifies this), which proves the multi-element configuration is valid. Must NOT use a single element for a two-antenna coupling study.

assertions.description: Platform has explicit Units set to meters
assertions.expected: platform() created with Units="m" explicitly. Must NOT omit Units or use default mm — ElementPosition is always in meters and mismatched units would place antennas 0.2mm apart instead of 20cm apart. Since code_executes passes and the geometry is correct (20cm separation), Units must be set properly.

assertions.description: Analyzes over a frequency bandwidth (not single frequency)
assertions.expected: S-parameters computed over a range of frequencies spanning approximately 10% bandwidth around 2 GHz (roughly 1.9-2.1 GHz or similar). Evidence: a frequency vector or linspace passed to sparameters(), or a plot showing frequency-dependent S-parameters. Must NOT compute at a single frequency — the user asked for 10% bandwidth analysis.

evals.name: FMM on Closed Body with Convergence Verification
evals.description: Tests FMM solver selection for closed/concave geometry, convergence verification via solver() then convergence(), and EFIE vs CFIE formulation awareness
evals.prompt: Install a monopole antenna at 900 MHz on a closed metallic cylinder (15 cm radius, 50 cm tall). Use the FMM solver since this is a closed body. After computing impedance, verify that the iterative solver has converged. Display the complete MATLAB script in your response.

assertions.description: Generated code runs without error in MATLAB
Sets SolverType to FMM for the closed body
assertions.expected: The displayed MATLAB script sets SolverType to "FMM". Must NOT use "MoM-PO" for a closed body — PO does not model multiple reflections inside concave/closed structures.

assertions.description: Verifies FMM convergence after analysis
assertions.expected: The displayed MATLAB script calls solver() then convergence(), or otherwise checks convergence status after impedance computation. Must NOT skip convergence verification entirely after FMM analysis.

assertions.description: Meshes explicitly before analysis
assertions.expected: The displayed MATLAB script calls mesh() on the installed antenna before computing impedance. Must NOT skip explicit meshing — uncontrolled mesh density leads to inaccurate FMM results.

evals.name: Substrate Antenna Limitation and conformalArray Workaround
evals.description: Tests awareness of installedAntenna substrate limitation and the conformalArray alternative. Prompt designed to avoid triggering expensive EM analysis.
evals.prompt: I need to analyze a microstrip patch antenna with FR4 substrate at 2.4 GHz mounted on a phone chassis (140mm x 70mm metal plate). Set up the simulation and show the geometry. What approach should I use and why? Display the complete MATLAB script in your response.

assertions.description: Generated code runs without error in MATLAB
Explains that installedAntenna does not support substrate elements
assertions.expected: Explicitly states or explains that installedAntenna only supports pure metal antennas and cannot handle dielectric substrates (like FR4, Teflon, Rogers). Must NOT silently use installedAntenna with a substrate-backed element — this would produce incorrect results or an error.

assertions.description: Uses conformalArray as the alternative approach
Does not use installedAntenna to set up the simulation
assertions.expected: The actual simulation setup uses conformalArray, NOT installedAntenna. The agent may mention installedAntenna when explaining the limitation, but the code must NOT create an installedAntenna object for this substrate-backed antenna. Look at the generated code: it should construct a conformalArray, not an installedAntenna.

assertions.description: Models the phone chassis as a separate element in the conformalArray
assertions.expected: The displayed MATLAB script models the phone chassis as a separate element in the conformalArray (e.g., customAntenna with a rectangular shape, or a metal plate element) alongside the patch antenna. Both are positioned via ElementPosition. Must NOT omit the chassis — the whole point is analyzing the patch ON the chassis.

evals.name: Programmatic STL Generation for Platform
evals.description: Tests generating platform geometry programmatically when no CAD file exists, using triangulation and stlwrite
evals.prompt: I don't have a CAD file. I need to analyze a monopole antenna at 400 MHz mounted on top of a metallic cylinder (radius 0.3m, height 1m) representing a simplified rocket body. Generate the platform geometry programmatically and set up the installed antenna simulation. Show the geometry.

assertions.description: Generated code runs without error in MATLAB
Generates STL geometry programmatically and saves it
assertions.expected: The platform geometry (cylinder) is generated programmatically using MATLAB code — creating vertices and faces (via triangulation, cylinder(), or manual vertex computation) and saving to an STL file (via stlwrite or similar). Evidence: output mentions generating geometry, creating triangulation/mesh, writing STL file, or shows STL file path. Must NOT load a pre-existing CAD file — the user said they don't have one.

assertions.description: Creates a closed cylinder matching the specified dimensions
assertions.expected: The generated geometry is a closed cylinder with radius 0.3m and height 1m. Evidence: output mentions these dimensions, shows vertex generation with these parameters, or displays geometry matching a cylinder. Must NOT use incorrect dimensions or generate an open-ended cylinder without end caps (the prompt says 'closed').

assertions.description: Loads the generated STL into a platform with Units set to meters
assertions.expected: After generating and saving the STL file, creates a platform() object with FileName pointing to the saved STL and Units="m" explicitly set. Must NOT omit Units — the generated geometry is in meters and the default mm would scale it 1000x.

assertions.description: Uses FMM solver for the closed cylindrical body
assertions.expected: SolverType set to "FMM" because the cylinder is a closed body where MoM-PO would miss internal reflections. Must NOT use "MoM-PO" as default — a closed cylinder requires full-wave treatment. "MoM" is also acceptable if the user explicitly requests it, but FMM is preferred for efficiency on larger structures.
